title: Two-colour shadow writing
subtitle: Writing the same text twice to create a coloured shadow effect
excerpt: Shadow writing lays a coloured or grey version of the text slightly offset behind the main black text. It was a standard decorative technique in Victorian copybooks and Spencerian display work. The method requires writing each line twice and maintaining consistent alignment between the passes.
sourceNotes: Shadow lettering is documented in Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866), and in Victorian commercial penmanship copybooks of the same era. Public domain.

glossaryTerms.definition: A calligraphic technique in which the same text is written twice: once in a lighter tone or colour offset slightly behind the main letter, to create the visual impression of a drop shadow cast by the letter.

content.text: creates depth by repeating each letter in a lighter tone, placed slightly behind the main letter. Victorian penmanship masters used it in display scripts and copybook headers. The effect shows a high level of control: the second pass must mirror the first at a consistent offset.

The standard offset is 1 to 2 mm, placed to the lower right of the main letter. This mimics a light source from the upper left, which is the conventional direction in European pictorial tradition. Placing the shadow to the lower left or above the letter reads as unconventional and draws attention to the technique rather than supporting the text.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 3 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-3)
items.description: Used for both main and shadow passes. Clean thoroughly between ink changes.

items.name: Calligraphy gouache (slug: calligraphy-gouache)
items.description: Diluted to ink consistency and tinted grey or a contrasting colour for the shadow pass. A small amount of black gouache in white gives a neutral grey; a small amount of the main colour in white gives a tinted shadow.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm (slug: cartridge-paper-220)
items.description: Heavyweight cartridge handles two ink passes without surface deterioration.

content.text: Before writing on the good sheet, plan the shadow offset on a tracing-paper dummy. Write a full line of text in pencil. Shift the paper 1.5 mm to the right and 1.5 mm downward and write the same line again. Check that the shadow pass reads clearly and is not so close to the main letter that the two merge.

Write the shadow pass first, before the main dark text. Working shadow-first means any small misalignments are corrected by the dark main letter on top, rather than appearing as gaps or overlaps in the finished piece. Mark the shadow guide-line position 1.5 mm below the main baseline and 1.5 mm to the right of the main left margin.
Load the nib with the shadow-colour gouache and write the text at normal speed and nib angle. Allow this pass to dry completely before writing the main text over it.

Switch to the main dark ink. Clean the nib thoroughly between shadow and main passes to prevent colour mixing. Write the main text on the standard guide lines, at the standard margins. The shadow should be visible below and to the right of each main letter.

items.symptom: Shadow and main letters merge into a blurred mass
items.cause: Offset too small for the nib size being used
items.fix: Increase the offset to 2 mm minimum. At nib-3, 1.5 mm is the lower limit; at nib-0, the minimum is 3 mm.

items.symptom: Shadow disappears behind the main letter on stems and thicks
items.cause: Main letter strokes too wide and covering the shadow
items.fix: This is inherent in the technique for vertical strokes. The shadow shows most clearly at the lower right of curves and arches, less on straight stems.
